text: Which of the following keywords can appear in a module-info.java file? (Choose all that apply.)

options.text: requires
exports
imports
opens
uses
provides

explanation: "imports" is not a module directive. The valid directives are: requires, exports, opens, uses, provides (with ... with).
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 12 – Modules, pp. 546–558

text: Which statement about unnamed modules is correct?

options.text: Unnamed modules can read from named modules but named modules cannot read from unnamed modules.
Unnamed modules can only be used on the module path.
There can only be one unnamed module per JVM.
An unnamed module exports all its packages to all other modules.
Unnamed modules require explicit requires declarations.

explanation: An unnamed module (classpath) exports all of its packages and reads all named modules. Named modules do not read from the unnamed module by default.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 12 – Modules, pp. 562–564

text: What is the difference between 'exports com.example' and 'opens com.example' in a module-info.java?

options.text: exports makes the package available at compile time and runtime; opens allows deep reflection at runtime only.
exports allows runtime reflection; opens allows compile-time access.
They are synonyms.
opens is used for service providers; exports is used for normal packages.

explanation: exports makes public types in the package accessible for both compilation and runtime use. opens allows other modules to use deep reflection (e.g., private field access via setAccessible) at runtime only — it does NOT expose types for compilation.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 12 – Modules, pp. 553–558

options.text: A module that is automatically generated by the JVM.
A JAR placed on the module path without a module-info.java.
A JAR placed on the classpath without a module-info.java.
A module that requires all other modules automatically.

explanation: An automatic module is a non-modular JAR placed on the module path. It gets a module name derived from the JAR filename (or MANIFEST.MF Automatic-Module-Name). It exports all its packages and reads all other modules. A JAR on the classpath (not module path) becomes part of the unnamed module.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 12 – Modules, pp. 560–562

text: Which of the following are true about the ServiceLoader API and module system? (Choose all that apply.)

options.text: A module that consumes a service declares 'uses ServiceInterface;'.
A module that provides a service declares 'provides ServiceInterface with ImplementationClass;'.
The service interface must be in the same module as the implementation.
ServiceLoader.load(ServiceInterface.class) discovers all available providers.
Service provider modules do not need to export the implementation package.

explanation: The consumer module uses 'uses' and the provider module uses 'provides...with'. The interface and implementation can be in different modules. ServiceLoader.load() discovers providers. The implementing class does not need to be in an exported package — the module system makes it available via the provides declaration.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 12 – Modules, pp. 558–562

text: Which command-line tool analyzes dependencies and can suggest module requirements?

options.text: jlink
jdeps
jmod
jpackage

explanation: jdeps analyzes the dependencies of a class, JAR, or module and can generate suggested module-info.java content. jlink creates a custom runtime image. jmod creates and inspects JMOD files. jpackage creates native installers.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 12 – Modules, pp. 565–568

text: What does the following module-info.java allow?

options.text: com.app.internal is accessible to all modules.
com.app.internal is only accessible to com.partner module.
com.app.api is only accessible to com.partner module.
java.sql is transitive — all modules that require com.app also get java.sql.

explanation: 'exports com.app.internal to com.partner' is a qualified export — only the com.partner module can access com.app.internal. com.app.api is exported to all. requires java.sql is not transitive (that would require 'requires transitive java.sql').
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 12 – Modules, pp. 549–553

text: Which tool is used to create a custom runtime image containing only the modules needed by an application?

options.text: jdeps
jmod
jlink
jar

explanation: jlink assembles a set of modules and their transitive dependencies into a custom runtime image. This reduces the size of the deployed JRE to only what the application needs.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 12 – Modules, pp. 568–570

text: What is 'requires transitive' used for in a module declaration?

options.text: It makes the required module's packages visible to all modules that require this module.
It allows deep reflection on the required module.
It is required when the dependency is optional at runtime.
It exports all packages from the required module.

explanation: 'requires transitive moduleName' creates an implied readability: any module that reads this module also implicitly reads the transitive dependency. This is used when the module's API types include types from the dependency — callers need to see those types too.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 12 – Modules, pp. 553–555

text: Which of the following statements about module-info.java are correct? (Choose all that apply.)

options.text: module-info.java must be placed in the root of the source tree for the module.
A module can have multiple module-info.java files.
The module name can contain dots but not hyphens.
module-info.java can declare a package.
A module must explicitly require java.base.

explanation: Each module has exactly one module-info.java at the source root. Module names can use dots (like package names) but hyphens are not valid Java identifiers. module-info.java cannot declare a package. java.base is always implicitly required — you do not need to declare it.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 12 – Modules, pp. 544–548

text: What is the purpose of 'requires static' in a module-info.java?

options.text: The dependency is required at compile time but optional at runtime.
The dependency is required at runtime but not compile time.
The dependency is always required and is loaded statically.
It marks the module as a static utility module.

explanation: 'requires static' declares a compile-time only dependency (optional at runtime). This is useful for annotation processors, code quality tools, or optional libraries that may not be present at runtime.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 12 – Modules, pp. 553–555

text: Which command compiles a module located at src/com.example with module-info.java?

options.text: javac --module-source-path src -d out --module com.example
javac -sourcepath src -d out com.example
javac --module com.example -d out
javac -module-path src -d out

explanation: The correct flag is --module-source-path to specify the source root for modules, combined with --module (or -m) to specify which module to compile. -d specifies the output directory.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 12 – Modules, pp. 538–544

text: Which of the following are true about the difference between automatic modules and the unnamed module? (Choose all that apply.)

options.text: Automatic modules have a module name; the unnamed module does not.
Named modules can declare 'requires' on an automatic module.
Named modules can declare 'requires' on the unnamed module.
Automatic modules can read the unnamed module.
Both automatic and unnamed modules export all their packages.

explanation: Automatic modules have a derived name, allowing named modules to 'requires' them. Named modules cannot 'requires' the unnamed module because it has no name. Automatic modules can access the unnamed module. Both types export all their packages automatically.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 12 – Modules, pp. 560–564

text: What does the following module descriptor compile error indicate?

options.text: Duplicate exports are silently ignored.
Compilation error: duplicate exports directive.
Only the first export is used; the second is ignored.
This is valid and exports the package twice for redundancy.

explanation: A module-info.java cannot have duplicate directives for the same package. Duplicate 'exports com.app.service' is a compilation error. Each package can only appear in one exports directive.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 12 – Modules, pp. 546–553

text: Which of the following is true about packages in a module that are NOT exported?

options.text: They are accessible to all modules at runtime but not at compile time.
They are strongly encapsulated — inaccessible to other modules at both compile and runtime.
They can be accessed by the unnamed module but not named modules.
They must be declared private to prevent access.

explanation: The module system enforces strong encapsulation. Packages not listed in an exports or opens directive are inaccessible to other modules at both compile time and runtime (even with reflection, unless opened). This is a key difference from the classpath where all classes are accessible.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 12 – Modules, pp. 533–535
